content.full_text:
FAIR POLITICAL p RACTICES COMMISSION 
428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329 
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886 
Frank Hotchkiss 
Council member 
City of Santa Barbara 
P O  Box 1990 
Santa Barbara, CA 93102-1990 
Re: 
Your Request for Advice 
Our File No. A-13-129 
Dear Mr. Hotchkiss: 
November 8, 2013 
This letter responds to your request for advice regarding the conflict-of-interest 
provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").1 This letter is based only on the facts 
presented. The Fair Political Practices Commission (the "Commission") does not act as a finder 
of fact when it renders advice. (In re Oglesby ( 1975) I FPPC Ops. 71.) 
Please note that our advice is based solely on the provisions of the Act. We therefore 
offer no opinion on the application, if any, of other conflict-of-interest laws, such as Government 
Code Section 1090, common law conflict of interest, or restrictions on incompatible activities 
your agency may impose. You should consult your agency's counsel regarding these, and any 
other laws, that may apply. 
QUESTION 
As a member of the Santa Barbara City Council may you participate in discussions, 
deliberations, and decisions regarding a potential amendment to the City of Santa Barbara's 
Zoning Information Report (ZIR) Ordinance when you are a licensed realtor? 
1 The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory 
references arc lo the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices 
Commission arc contained in Sections 181 lO through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All 
regulatory references arc to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated. 


CONCLUSION 
File No. A-13-129 
Page No. 2 
Yes, you may participate in discussions, deliberations, and decisions regarding a potential 
amendment to the City of Santa Barbara's ZIR Ordinance if there will not be a reasonably 
foreseeable material financial effect upon any of your economic interests. 
FACTS 
You are a member of the Santa Barbara City Council. You are also a realtor licensed to 
sell real estate in California. You work with Sotheby's International Realty Company 
specializing in the sale of residential real estate and sell an average of 2-3 properties per year 
within the City of Santa Barbara. Your income from sales of property comes directly from your 
clients, not Sotheby's and you have no investment interest in Sotheby's. Sotheby's as a broker 
collects a portion of the income you receive from sales of properties. You are also an active 
member of the Santa Barbara Association of Realtors (hereinafter "SBA OR.'') 
The City of Santa Barbara has a zoning ordinance that requires sellers of single family 
residential properties to apply to the City for the preparation of a Zoning Information Report 
(ZIR). The ordinance requires the seller to provide the ZIR prepared by City staff to the 
prospective buyer prior to the transfer of title on the residential property. A ZIR consists of the 
following information: 
I. Street address and parcel number of the property. 
2. The zone classification and permitted used as set forth in the Zoning Ordinance of the 
City of Santa Barbara. 
3. The occupancy and use permitted as indicated and established by City records. 
4. Variance, special use permits, conditional use permits, modifications and other 
administrative acts of record. 
5. Any special restrictions in use or development which are recorded in City records and 
may apply to the property. 
6. Any known nonconformities or violations of any ordinances or law. 
7. The results of a physical inspection for compliance with the Zoning Ordinance and 
for compliance with Chapter 14.46 of this Code. 
8. A statement of whether the real property has had a Building Sewer Lateral Report 
prepared for the real property pursuant to the requirements of Santa Barbara 
Municipal Code. 
The current fee for the preparation of a ZIR is about $465. 
The Santa Barbara Association of Realtors (SBA OR) has requested that the City Council 
either repeal the ZIR ordinance or amend the ordinance to make the application for and 
disclosure of ZIR's optional or voluntary on the home seller's part. In SBAOR's request to the 
City they cite examples where property owner sellers have purchased properties with "clean" 
ZIR's (a report that does not identify any zoning violations) but then are later required by the 


File No. A-13-129 
Page No. 3 
City to remediate zoning and building code violations that are discovered upon the preparation of 
a new ZIR for a subsequent sale of the real property. The SBA OR also points to the cost of 
preparing ZIRs as being a point of contention for Realtors and their clients, particularly sellers. 
In addition to the SBAOR request, individual local realtors have submitted letters about 
home sale transactions where the remediation of zoning violations discovered in subsequent 
ZIRs have cost their clients selling residences tens of thousands of dollars and in some instances 
resulted in the cancellation of the sales and listing agreement for the home. 
The Santa Barbara City Council is scheduled to discuss and consider amendments to the 
City's ZIR ordinance as requested by the SBOAR this fall and possibly in early 2014. Your 
question is whether you may participate in the City Council's discussions, deliberations, and 
decisions regarding a potential amendment of the City's ZIR ordinance? 
ANALYSIS 
The Act's conflict-of-interest rules prohibit a public official from making, participating in 
making, or using his or her official position in any way to influence a governmental decision in 
which the official knows, or has reason to know, that he or she has a "financial interest." 
(Section 87100.) Section 87103 provides that a public official has a "financial interest" in a 
governmental decision if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material 
financial effect, distinguishable from its effect on the public generally, on the official, a member 
of his or her immediate family, or on any of the official's interests specified in Section 87103. 
Under the Act, a conflict of interest exists only when a public official has a financial 
interest in a particular governmental decision. To determine whether a public official has a 
"conflict of interest" in a specific governmental decision, we employ a standard eight-step 
analysis outlined in Regulation l 8700(b ). 
You have stated that there is a decision regarding a change to the Santa Barbara ZIR 
ordinance and as a Santa Barbara City Council member you are a public official under the Act. 
Step 3: What are your Economic Interests? 
A public official has a financial interest in a decision within the meaning of Section 
87103 if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial effect, 
distinguishable from its effect on the public generally, on the official, a member of his or her 
immediate family, or on any one of five enumerated interests. (Section 87103; Regulations 
18703-18703.5.) 
The applicable interests include: 
• An interest in a business entity in which a public official has a direct or indirect 
investment of $2.000 or more. (Section 87103(a), Regulation 18703. l (a).) 


File No. A-13-129 
Page No. 4 
• 
An interest in any business entity in which a public official is a director, officer, partner, 
trustee, employee, or holds any position of management. (Section 87 l03(d), Regulation 
18703. l(b).) 
• 
An interest in real property in which a public official has a direct or indirect interest of 
$2,000 or more. (Section 87103(b), Regulation 18703.2.) 
• 
Any source of income, including promised income, to the public official that aggregates 
to $500 or more within 12 months prior to the decision. (Section 87103(c), Regulation 
18703.3.) 
• 
Any source of gifts to the public official if the gifts aggregate to $440 or more within 12 
months prior to the decision. (Section 87 l03(e), Regulation l 8703.4.) 
• 
A public official also has an interest in his or her personal expenses, income, assets, or 
liabilities, as well as those of his or her immediate family. This is also known as the 
"personal financial effects" rule. (Section 87103, Regulation 18703.5.) 
You have stated that you are a licensed real estate agent and that you work with 
Sotheby's International Realty Company. Under Regulation 18703.3 sources of income for a 
real estate agent include the broker and brokerage business entity under whose auspices the agent 
works, the person the agent represents in the transaction, and any person who receives a finder's 
or other referral fee for referring a party to the transaction with the broker, or who makes a 
referral pursuant to contract with the broker. 
Therefore, you have an economic interest in Sotheby's International Realty Company, the 
clients you represent in real estate transactions from which you receive income and any person 
who receives a finders or other referral fee for referring a transaction to your broker. The full 
gross value of any commission income for a specific sale or similar transaction shall be 
attributed to each source of income in that sale or transaction. 
In addition, a public official always has an economic interest in his or her personal 
finances, including those of his or her immediate family. This is the "personal financial effects" 
rule. (Section 87103; Regulation 18703.5.) 
Step 4: Will your economic interests be directly or indirectly involved in the decisions you 
will make, participate in making, or influence as a public official? 
Sources of Income and Business Emities - Regulation 18704.1 (a) states that sources of 
income and business entities are directly involved in a decision before the official's agency when 
that source of income or business entity, either directly or by an agent: 
"( 1) Initiates the proceeding in which the decision will be made by filing 
an application, claim, appeal, or similar request or; 
"(2) Is a named party in, or is the subject of, the proceeding concerning 
the decision before the official or the official's agency. A person is the subject of 
a proceeding if a decision involves the issuance, renewal, approval, denial or 


File No. A-13-129 
Page No. 5 
revocation of any license, permit, or other entitlement to, or contract with, the 
subject person." 
Sources of income that are not directly involved in governmental decisions are regarded 
as indirectly involved. (Regulations l 8704(a} and 18704. l (b ).) 
Though it is likely that a person that has been a source of income to you would be 
affected by the upcoming decision, you have not stated that any of your clients, Sotheby's, or 
other party that refers transactions to Sotheby's has initiated the proceeding or is a named party 
in or the subject of the proceeding. Therefore, your economic interests in your clients, 
Sotheby's, or other party that refers transactions to Sotheby's are indirectly involved in the 
upcoming governmental decision. 
Personal Finances - An official's economic interest in his or her personal finances is 
deemed to be directly involved in the governmental decision if facts suggest any financial effect 
on the economic interest. (Regulation 18704.5.) 
Step 5: \Vhat is the applicable materiality standard? 
Sources of Income - Regulation 18705.3(b)(3) provides the materiality standards for 
interests in persons, other than business entitie!., who are sources of income to the public official 
and are indirectly involved in a governmental decision. For an indirectly involved source of 
income, who is an individual, the effect of a decision is material if any of the following apply: 
"(A) The decision will affect the individual's income, investments, or other 
tangible or intangible assets or liabilities ( other than real property) by $1,000 or 
more; or 
"(B) The decision will affect the individual's real property interest in a manner 
that is considered material under [Regulation] 18705.2(b ). " 
Regulation 18705 .2(b) provides: 
" ... The financial effect of a governmental decision on real property which is indirectly 
involved in the governmental decision is presumed not to be material. This presumption 
may be rebutted by proof that there are specific circumstances regarding the 
governmental decision, its financial effect, and the nature of the real property in which 
the public official has an economic interest, which make it reasonably foreseeable that the 
decision will have a material financial effect on the real property in which the public 
official has an interest. Examples of specific circumstances that will be considered 
include, but are not limited to, circumstances where the decision affects: 
(A) The development potential or income producing potential of the real property 
in which the official has an economic interest; 


File No. A-13-129 
Page No. 6 
(B) The use of the real property in which the official has an economic interest; 
(C) The character of the neighborhood including, but not limited to, substantial 
effects on: traffic, view, privacy, intensity of use, noise levels, air emissions, or similar 
traits of the neighborhood." 
If the reasonably foreseeable financial effect on any of your interests meets any of the 
above thresholds, the financial effect of a decision is deemed material. 
Bwiiness Entities - Regulation 18705. l (c) ( copy enclosed) states the materiality 
standards for indirectly involved business entities. Whether a financial effect upon an indirectly 
involved business entity is material depends upon the net income of the business in the most 
recent fiscal year and whether the governmental decision will have an effect upon gross 
revenues, expenses, value of assets and liabilities of a certain amount. For example, if 
Sotheby's has a net income of no less than $750,000 in the most recent fiscal year, the financial 
effect upon Sotheby's would be material if it is reasonably foreseeable that the governmental 
decision will result in an increase or decrease to Sotheby's gross revenues for the fiscal year of 
$300,000 or more, will result in Sotheby's incurring or avoiding additional expenses or reducing 
or eliminating expenses of $100,000 or more for the fiscal year, or will result in an increase or 
decrease in value of assets or liabilities of $300,000 or more in.the fiscal year for Sotheby's. 
Because you have not provided the net income of Sotheby's in the most recent fiscal year, 
please refer to Regulation 18705.1 for the applicable materiality standard. 
Personal Finances - An effect on an official's personal finances is material as stated in 
Regulation 18705.S(a), which provides, "A reasonably foreseeable financial effect on a public 
official's personal finances is material if it is at least $250 in any 12-month period." 
Step 6: Is the material financial effect reasonably foreseeable? 
An official has a conflict of interest in a governmental decision only if a material 
financial effect is reasonably foreseeable. A financial effect need not be certain or even 
substantially likely to be reasonably foreseeable, but it must be more than a mere possibility. 
(Tann Advice Letter, A-12-134; Norton Advice Letter, 1-09-091; Norton Advice Letter, 1-12-
092.) 
The concept of foreseeability is heavily driven by the particular facts of each situation, as 
they exist at the time the decision is made. The determination of whether it is reasonably 
foreseeable that the applicable materiality standard will be met for your interests is necessarily a 
factual question that is ultimately one for you to make based on your understanding of the facts. 
It appears that there will not be a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect upon 
your sources of income who are individuals as a result of the upcoming decision. The cost of a 
ZIR is approximately $465, whereas Regulation 18705.3(b)(3)(A) states that in order for a 
financial effect to be material it must affect the individual's assets by $1,000 or more. 


File No. A-13-129 
Page No. 7 
Additionally, you have not stated whether the actual ZIR affects the value of properties, 
you have stated that subsequent ZIR's have affected sales, but it is not the ZIR itself, but rather 
the restrictions that are already placed upon the properties that affect the values of the properties 
not the ZIR itself. Therefore, there would not be a reasonably material financial effect upon your 
sources of income under Regulation 18705.3(b)(3)(B) either. 
You have not provided any facts to indicate that there will be a reasonably foreseeable 
material financial effect upon Sotheby's as a result of this decision. Again, please refer to 
Regulation 18705. l(c) for applicable materiality standards. If you believe one of the materiality 
standards may be met, please seek further assistance. 
Steps Seven and Eight: Does the governmental decision come within any exception to the 
conflict-of-interest rules? 
Even if an official has a conflict of interest, disqualification is not required if the 
governmental decision affects the official's interests in a manner that is indistinguishable from 
the manner in which the decision will affect the public generally. (Section 87103; Regulation 
l 8707(a).) Additionally, in certain rare circumstances, an official may be called upon to take 
part in a decision despite the fact that the official may have a disqualifying conflict of interest 
under the "legally required participation" exception. This exception applies only in certain very 
specific circumstances where the government agency would be paralyzed from acting. (Section 
87 10 I; Regulation 18708.) 
However, you have not presented any facts indicating that the "public generally" or the 
"legally required participation" exceptions are applicable to your circumstances, so we do not 
address them further. 
If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (9 I 6) 322-5660. 
SKB:jgl 
Sincerely, 
Zackery P. Morazzini 
General Counsel 
By: 
Sukhi K. Brar 
Counsel, Legal Division

sections.question:
As a member of the Santa Barbara City Council may you participate in discussions, 
deliberations, and decisions regarding a potential amendment to the City of Santa Barbara's 
Zoning Information Report (ZIR) Ordinance when you are a licensed realtor? 
The regulations of the Fair Political Practices 
Commission arc contained in Sections 181 lO through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations.
sections.conclusion:
Yes, you may participate in discussions, deliberations, and decisions regarding a potential 
amendment to the City of Santa Barbara's ZIR Ordinance if there will not be a reasonably 
foreseeable material financial effect upon any of your economic interests.

sections.analysis:
The Act's conflict-of-interest rules prohibit a public official from making, participating in 
making, or using his or her official position in any way to influence a governmental decision in 
which the official knows, or has reason to know, that he or she has a "financial interest." 
(Section 87100.) Section 87103 provides that a public official has a "financial interest" in a 
governmental decision if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material 
financial effect, distinguishable from its effect on the public generally, on the official, a member 
of his or her immediate family, or on any of the official's interests specified in Section 87103. 
Under the Act, a conflict of interest exists only when a public official has a financial 
interest in a particular governmental decision. To determine whether a public official has a 
"conflict of interest" in a specific governmental decision, we employ a standard eight-step 
analysis outlined in Regulation l 8700(b ). 
You have stated that there is a decision regarding a change to the Santa Barbara ZIR 
ordinance and as a Santa Barbara City Council member you are a public official under the Act. 
Step 3: What are your Economic Interests? 
A public official has a financial interest in a decision within the meaning of Section 
87103 if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial effect, 
distinguishable from its effect on the public generally, on the official, a member of his or her 
immediate family, or on any one of five enumerated interests. (Section 87103; Regulations 
18703-18703.5.) 
The applicable interests include: 
• An interest in a business entity in which a public official has a direct or indirect 
investment of $2.000 or more. (Section 87103(a), Regulation 18703. l (a).)  
• 
An interest in any business entity in which a public official is a director, officer, partner, 
trustee, employee, or holds any position of management. (Section 87 l03(d), Regulation 
18703. l(b).) 
• 
An interest in real property in which a public official has a direct or indirect interest of 
$2,000 or more. (Section 87103(b), Regulation 18703.2.) 
• 
Any source of income, including promised income, to the public official that aggregates 
to $500 or more within 12 months prior to the decision. (Section 87103(c), Regulation 
18703.3.) 
• 
Any source of gifts to the public official if the gifts aggregate to $440 or more within 12 
months prior to the decision. (Section 87 l03(e), Regulation l 8703.4.) 
• 
A public official also has an interest in his or her personal expenses, income, assets, or 
liabilities, as well as those of his or her immediate family. This is also known as the 
"personal financial effects" rule. (Section 87103, Regulation 18703.5.) 
You have stated that you are a licensed real estate agent and that you work with 
Sotheby's International Realty Company. Under Regulation 18703.3 sources of income for a 
real estate agent include the broker and brokerage business entity under whose auspices the agent 
works, the person the agent represents in the transaction, and any person who receives a finder's 
or other referral fee for referring a party to the transaction with the broker, or who makes a 
referral pursuant to contract with the broker. 
Therefore, you have an economic interest in Sotheby's International Realty Company, the 
clients you represent in real estate transactions from which you receive income and any person 
who receives a finders or other referral fee for referring a transaction to your broker. The full 
gross value of any commission income for a specific sale or similar transaction shall be 
attributed to each source of income in that sale or transaction. 
In addition, a public official always has an economic interest in his or her personal 
finances, including those of his or her immediate family. This is the "personal financial effects" 
rule. (Section 87103; Regulation 18703.5.) 
Step 4: Will your economic interests be directly or indirectly involved in the decisions you 
will make, participate in making, or influence as a public official? 
Sources of Income and Business Emities - Regulation 18704.1 (a) states that sources of 
income and business entities are directly involved in a decision before the official's agency when 
that source of income or business entity, either directly or by an agent: 
"( 1) Initiates the proceeding in which the decision will be made by filing 
an application, claim, appeal, or similar request or; 
"(2) Is a named party in, or is the subject of, the proceeding concerning 
the decision before the official or the official's agency. A person is the subject of 
a proceeding if a decision involves the issuance, renewal, approval, denial or  
revocation of any license, permit, or other entitlement to, or contract with, the 
subject person." 
Sources of income that are not directly involved in governmental decisions are regarded 
as indirectly involved. (Regulations l 8704(a} and 18704. l (b ).) 
Though it is likely that a person that has been a source of income to you would be 
affected by the upcoming decision, you have not stated that any of your clients, Sotheby's, or 
other party that refers transactions to Sotheby's has initiated the proceeding or is a named party 
in or the subject of the proceeding. Therefore, your economic interests in your clients, 
Sotheby's, or other party that refers transactions to Sotheby's are indirectly involved in the 
upcoming governmental decision. 
Personal Finances - An official's economic interest in his or her personal finances is 
deemed to be directly involved in the governmental decision if facts suggest any financial effect 
on the economic interest. (Regulation 18704.5.) 
Step 5: \Vhat is the applicable materiality standard? 
Sources of Income - Regulation 18705.3(b)(3) provides the materiality standards for

and are indirectly involved in a governmental decision. For an indirectly involved source of

"(A) The decision will affect the individual's income, investments, or other

more; or 
"(B) The decision will affect the individual's real property interest in a manner 
that is considered material under [Regulation] 18705.2(b ). " 
Regulation 18705 .2(b) provides: 
" ... The financial effect of a governmental decision on real property which is indirectly 
involved in the governmental decision is presumed not to be material. This presumption 
may be rebutted by proof that there are specific circumstances regarding the 
governmental decision, its financial effect, and the nature of the real property in which 
the public official has an economic interest, which make it reasonably foreseeable that the

official has an interest. Examples of specific circumstances that will be considered 
include, but are not limited to, circumstances where the decision affects: 
(A) The development potential or income producing potential of the real property 
in which the official has an economic interest;  
(B) The use of the real property in which the official has an economic interest; 
(C) The character of the neighborhood including, but not limited to, substantial

Bwiiness Entities - Regulation 18705. l (c) ( copy enclosed) states the materiality

$300,000 or more, will result in Sotheby's incurring or avoiding additional expenses or reducing 
or eliminating expenses of $100,000 or more for the fiscal year, or will result in an increase or 
decrease in value of assets or liabilities of $300,000 or more in.the fiscal year for Sotheby's. 
Because you have not provided the net income of Sotheby's in the most recent fiscal year, 
please refer to Regulation 18705.1 for the applicable materiality standard. 
Personal Finances - An effect on an official's personal finances is material as stated in 
Regulation 18705.S(a), which provides, "A reasonably foreseeable financial effect on a public 
official's personal finances is material if it is at least $250 in any 12-month period." 
Step 6: Is the material financial effect reasonably foreseeable? 
An official has a conflict of interest in a governmental decision only if a material 
financial effect is reasonably foreseeable. A financial effect need not be certain or even 
substantially likely to be reasonably foreseeable, but it must be more than a mere possibility. 
(Tann Advice Letter, A-12-134; Norton Advice Letter, 1-09-091; Norton Advice Letter, 1-12-
092.) 
The concept of foreseeability is heavily driven by the particular facts of each situation, as 
they exist at the time the decision is made. The determination of whether it is reasonably 
foreseeable that the applicable materiality standard will be met for your interests is necessarily a 
factual question that is ultimately one for you to make based on your understanding of the facts. 
It appears that there will not be a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect upon 
your sources of income who are individuals as a result of the upcoming decision. The cost of a 
ZIR is approximately $465, whereas Regulation 18705.3(b)(3)(A) states that in order for a 
financial effect to be material it must affect the individual's assets by $1,000 or more.  
Additionally, you have not stated whether the actual ZIR affects the value of properties, 
you have stated that subsequent ZIR's have affected sales, but it is not the ZIR itself, but rather 
the restrictions that are already placed upon the properties that affect the values of the properties 
not the ZIR itself. Therefore, there would not be a reasonably material financial effect upon your 
sources of income under Regulation 18705.3(b)(3)(B) either. 
You have not provided any facts to indicate that there will be a reasonably foreseeable 
material financial effect upon Sotheby's as a result of this decision. Again, please refer to 
Regulation 18705. l(c) for applicable materiality standards. If you believe one of the materiality 
standards may be met, please seek further assistance. 
Steps Seven and Eight: Does the governmental decision come within any exception to the 
conflict-of-interest rules? 
Even if an official has a conflict of interest, disqualification is not required if the 
governmental decision affects the official's interests in a manner that is indistinguishable from 
the manner in which the decision will affect the public generally. (Section 87103; Regulation

87 10 I; Regulation 18708.)

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: As a member of the Santa Barbara City Council may you participate in discussions, 
deliberations, and decisions regarding a potential amendment to the City of Santa Barbara's 
Zoning Information Report (ZIR) Ordinance when you are a licensed realtor? 
The regulations of the Fair Political Practices 
Commission arc contained in Sections 181 lO through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations.

CONCLUSION: Yes, you may participate in discussions, deliberations, and decisions regarding a potential 
amendment to the City of Santa Barbara's ZIR Ordinance if there will not be a reasonably 
foreseeable material financial effect upon any of your economic interests.
embedding.first_500_words: FAIR POLITICAL p RACTICES COMMISSION 428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329 (916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886 Frank Hotchkiss Council member City of Santa Barbara P O Box 1990 Santa Barbara, CA 93102-1990 Re: Your Request for Advice Our File No. A-13-129 Dear Mr. Hotchkiss: November 8, 2013 This letter responds to your request for advice regarding the conflict-of-interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").1 This letter is based only on the facts presented. The Fair Political Practices Commission (the "Commission") does not act as a finder of fact when it renders advice. (In re Oglesby ( 1975) I FPPC Ops. 71.) Please note that our advice is based solely on the provisions of the Act. We therefore offer no opinion on the application, if any, of other conflict-of-interest laws, such as Government Code Section 1090, common law conflict of interest, or restrictions on incompatible activities your agency may impose. You should consult your agency's counsel regarding these, and any other laws, that may apply. QUESTION As a member of the Santa Barbara City Council may you participate in discussions, deliberations, and decisions regarding a potential amendment to the City of Santa Barbara's Zoning Information Report (ZIR) Ordinance when you are a licensed realtor? 1 The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory references arc lo the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices Commission arc contained in Sections 181 lO through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All regulatory references arc to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated. CONCLUSION File No. A-13-129 Page No. 2 Yes, you may participate in discussions, deliberations, and decisions regarding a potential amendment to the City of Santa Barbara's ZIR Ordinance if there will not be a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect upon any of your economic interests. FACTS You are a member of the Santa Barbara City Council. You are also a realtor licensed to sell real estate in California. You work with Sotheby's International Realty Company specializing in the sale of residential real estate and sell an average of 2-3 properties per year within the City of Santa Barbara. Your income from sales of property comes directly from your clients, not Sotheby's and you have no investment interest in Sotheby's. Sotheby's as a broker collects a portion of the income you receive from sales of properties. You are also an active member of the Santa Barbara Association of Realtors (hereinafter "SBA OR.'') The City of Santa Barbara has a zoning ordinance that requires sellers of single family residential properties to apply to the City for the preparation of a Zoning Information Report (ZIR). The ordinance requires the seller to provide the ZIR prepared by City staff to the prospective buyer prior to the transfer of title on the residential property. A ZIR consists of the following information: I. Street address and parcel number
